DEMOCRATIC CAUCUS SECRETARY 
June 12, 1980 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
Post Office Box 807 
Sacramento, California 95804 
Dear Bob: 
COMMIITEES, 
HEALTH 
FINANCE, INSURANCE 
AND COMMERCE 
GOVERNMENTAL ORGANIZATION 
COMMIITEE ON AGING 
I would be most grateful if you will confirm in writing our telephone 
conversation of last week in which you answered ~ question about the 
propriety and legality of using campaign funds to help finance the travel 
of my wife with me on a trip to Greece in July, 1980. 
As I stated, I have been extended an official invitation of the 
Greek government to visit Greece as their guest for ten days beginning 
July 26. 
The Greek government will underwrite all of my expenses for this 
trip to learn more about the problems of Greece as they relate to the 
United States. 
However, the participation and attendance of ~ wife would be extremely 
valuable but impossible for the Greek government to underwrite. 
Since I cannot afford to pay for it with personal funds, I am seeking 
your official guidance on the propriety and legality of using my c~Jaign 
funds to help underwrite the travel expenses for my w' e. 
AA:deb

The term "rate-making" does not include

tion by a public utility which is challenging the 
board's determination of the assessed valuation 
of its property.

sections.question: whether a campaign committee can make expenditures for travel expenses to Greece on behalf of a public official's wife who would accompany the official on an official government-sponsored trip
sections.conclusion: The Political Reform Act as amended does not restrict the use of campaign funds in a manner inconsistent with the law. Campaign expenditures must be reported on the campaign statement, and any use of funds must be for proper purposes.
sections.question_synthetic: Can a California state legislator use campaign funds to help finance travel expenses for his wife to accompany him on an official trip to Greece sponsored by the Greek government?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC's response indicates that campaign fund usage is subject to reporting requirements and must comply with the Political Reform Act, though the specific document is degraded and the complete conclusion is not fully legible.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document quality is poor with significant text degradation and illegibility. The incoming request (June 12 letter from Agnos) is clearly legible, but the FPPC response letter (June 13) is heavily redacted or damaged, making extraction of the complete conclusion difficult. The document appears to contain both the incoming request and FPPC response. A separate memo regarding Section 82002 and rate-making is also included but appears unrelated to the main inquiry.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: whether a campaign committee can make expenditures for travel expenses to Greece on behalf of a public official's wife who would accompany the official on an official government-sponsored trip

CONCLUSION: The Political Reform Act as amended does not restrict the use of campaign funds in a manner inconsistent with the law. Campaign expenditures must be reported on the campaign statement, and any use of funds must be for proper purposes.

embedding.summary: This is an FPPC advice letter responding to Assemblyman Art Agnos's inquiry regarding the legality of using campaign funds to finance his wife's travel expenses on a Greek government-sponsored official trip in July 1980.
